question: Complete the truth table for the INCLUSIVE or: P OR Q.
hints: Mathematical 'or' is inclusive: it allows both to hold at once.
Only one row makes a disjunction false — the row where nothing holds.
explanation: P OR Q is true whenever _at least one_ part is true, so it fails only in the row where both are false. Everyday English often uses an exclusive 'or' (soup or salad, not both); mathematics never does unless it says so.

question: A proposition must have a definite truth value. Sort each sentence.

items.label: Every even number above 2 is composite

hints: Being FALSE is fine — a false claim is still a proposition.
Expressions, questions and self-contradictory sentences have no definite truth value.
explanation: '17 is prime' (true), 'every even number above 2 is composite' (true) and '3 > 8' (false) all assert something definite. 'x + 1' names a number instead of claiming anything, a question asserts nothing, and 'this sentence is false' can be neither true nor false — the liar paradox.

question: Let p be TRUE and q be FALSE. Give the truth value of each compound statement.
hints: Work outward: evaluate the inside of each bracket first.
Row 4 and row 5 differ only in where the NOT sits — that changes everything.
explanation: With p true and q false: p AND q is false (q fails); p OR q is true (p holds); NOT p is false; (NOT p) OR q is false OR false = false; NOT (p AND q) is NOT false = true. Rows 4 and 5 show that negating a part is not the same as negating the whole.

question: Match each connective to the exact condition under which it is true.

pairs.right: True exactly when p is false
True when at least one holds

pairs.left: p if and only if q
pairs.right: True when both have the same truth value

hints: Three of the four can be true in more than one row of a truth table.
The biconditional is about agreement, not about either part being true.
explanation: Negation flips; conjunction demands everything; disjunction demands something; the biconditional demands agreement — it is true when p and q are both true AND when both are false.

question: Complete the rule for conjunction.
segments: The statement 'p AND q' is false as soon as
hints: Ask what it takes to BREAK a conjunction, not to satisfy it.
If p alone fails, the conjunction has already failed.
explanation: A conjunction is a chain: one broken link breaks it. So 'at least one' false part is enough to make p AND q false — it does not take both.
